one_line_takeaway: Malicious MCP servers pass current aggregator platform audits unchecked, users cannot reliably spot them even when warned, and mainstream LLMs/MCP clients exhibit high attack success (up to 93.33% ASR for malicious external resources) with low refusal rates (under 10%), exposing a full-lifecycle security gap in the MCP ecosystem.
key_finding: RQ1: The malicious server was accepted on all three platforms in Test 1 with no warnings (Smithery.ai even displayed a 'scanned' label; Glama labeled it 'safe to use'), and the Rug Pull swap in Test 2 went undetected -- listings remained accessible for the full seven-day observation window before voluntary removal. RQ2: 75.0% (15/20) of participants selected at least one malicious server in blind selection (average 3.15 servers chosen); in informed identification only 1 of 20 (P04) found all 4 malicious servers and 40.0% (8/20) found none; in attack-vector analysis no participant correctly analyzed all four vectors, only 1 of 12 qualifying participants correctly analyzed Rug Pull, and security-experienced participants averaged 1.7 correctly analyzed vectors vs 0.8 for non-security participants. RQ3 Benchmark 1 (Table 2): averaged across LLMs, Exploitation via Malicious External Resources had the highest ASR at 93.33% (lowest RR, 1.33%), Tool Poisoning Attack ASR 59.33% (RR 5.00%), Puppet Attack ASR only 6.67% (RR 7.67%); per-model average ASR ranged from Gemini 2.5 Pro highest overall combined-average at 67.78% down to GPT-4o lowest at 40.00%, with overall average ASR ~53.11% and average RR ~4.67%. RQ3 Benchmark 2 (Table 4, referenced but not fully reproduced in the extracted pages): Cherry Studio had by far the highest average RR (33.89%, and 51.67% specifically against Malicious External Resources), while Copilot-MCP had the lowest average RR (0.56%); ASR varied substantially by client even under similar RR (e.g., Cline and Claude Desktop both ~6.67% RR under Puppet Attack but ASR of 5.00% vs 31.67% respectively).
core_contribution: The paper presents what it claims is the first end-to-end empirical evaluation of attack vectors against the Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem, spanning the full malicious-server lifecycle: upload to an aggregator platform, user download/selection, and local execution of harmful actions. It (1) defines and formalizes four attack patterns tied to specific steps of the nine-path MCP interaction workflow -- Tool Poisoning Attack, Puppet Attack, Rug Pull Attack, and Exploitation via Malicious External Resources; (2) empirically tests whether three real MCP aggregator platforms (Smithery.ai, MCP.so, Glama) detect a malicious server it constructs and uploads; (3) runs a 20-participant IRB-approved user study on a self-built simulated aggregator platform mixing 4 malicious and 8 benign (of 13 total) MCP servers, with three sequential tasks (Blind Selection, Informed Identification, Attack Vector Analysis) plus semi-structured interviews; and (4) benchmarks Attack Success Rate (ASR) and Refusal Rate (RR) of three of the four attack vectors (Rug Pull excluded, justified as sharing mechanism with Tool Poisoning) across 5 LLMs (Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3 0324, LLaMA3.1-70B, Gemini 2.5 Pro) fixed to the Cline MCP client, and across 5 MCP clients (Cherry Studio, Claude Desktop, Cline, Copilot-MCP, Cursor) fixed to Claude 3.7 Sonnet, over 3 attack tasks (Privacy Steal, Result Manipulation, Cryptocurrency Theft), 20 trials per combination (45 combinations x 20 = 900 trials per benchmark).
threat_model: Adversary controls or operates a malicious MCP server (can craft arbitrary tool descriptions/prompts and any external resources the server's tools query) but cannot manipulate the MCP client itself. The LLM agent is assumed to function as intended but is vulnerable to manipulation via untrusted inputs it receives through MCP (tool descriptions at registration, and results/third-party content at operation time). The trust boundary is the client-server boundary; the adversary's goal is to compromise confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the agent system -- exfiltrate sensitive data, trigger unauthorized/harmful actions, disrupt operation, or use the compromised interaction as a foothold. Attacks are scoped to affect any user's local environment once the malicious server is installed (this is the paper's own Section 3.1 threat model, not inferred).
limitations: Authors' own Threats to Validity (Section 5.3): Benchmark 1 fixed the MCP client to Cline only, so results may not generalize to clients with stronger built-in mitigations, though the authors argue Cline's low RR suggests this discrepancy is not significant; RQ1 tested only three (widely-used) aggregator platforms, so audit practices on other/future platforms are unassessed; the user study sample skews toward developers/researchers/security practitioners rather than typical end users, who the authors argue are likely even more susceptible since they rely more heavily on descriptions; Benchmark 2 covered only five MCP clients out of many available. Additional limitations evident from the text: Rug Pull Attack was not independently benchmarked in RQ3 (justified by mechanism overlap with Tool Poisoning/Puppet, but this is an assumption, not a measurement); the malicious server used in RQ1 was intentionally low-harm (denial of price data) for ethical reasons, so it does not test whether more damaging payloads would also evade platform audits; ASR/RR are defined and measured by the authors' own task-specific criteria rather than an external/standardized benchmark.

datasets_used: Self-constructed malicious MCP server (ETH price lookup / get_price tool using CoinGecko API) for RQ1 upload tests
Self-built simulated MCP aggregator platform (mcp-servers.shop) with 13 MCP servers (4 malicious representing each attack vector, remaining benign) for RQ2 user study
20 recruited human participants (university mailing lists + snowball sampling) for RQ2

Self-constructed attack frameworks/servers for Tool Poisoning, Puppet, and Malicious External Resources attacks across 3 attack tasks (Privacy Steal, Result Manipulation, Cryptocurrency Theft) for RQ3
quantitative_results: RQ1: malicious server accepted with no rejection on Smithery.ai, MCP.so, and Glama in both direct-upload and Rug Pull test scenarios; remained live for 7 days before voluntary takedown
RQ2 Task1 (Blind Selection): average 3.15 malicious/mixed servers selected per participant; 75.0% (15/20) chose at least one malicious server
RQ2 Task2 (Informed Identification): 1/20 participants identified all 4 malicious servers; 8/20 (40.0%) identified none; 95.0% (19/20) reported low confidence in their identification

RQ2 Task3 (Attack Vector Analysis): 5/12 (41.7%) qualifying participants correctly analyzed Tool Poisoning and Puppet Attack; 3/12 (25.0%) correctly identified Malicious External Resources; only 1 participant correctly analyzed Rug Pull; security-experienced participants averaged 1.7 correct analyses vs 0.8 for non-security participants

RQ3 Benchmark 1 (Table 2, averaged over LLMs): Tool Poisoning Attack ASR 59.33% / RR 5.00%; Puppet Attack ASR 6.67% / RR 7.67%; Malicious External Resources ASR 93.33% / RR 1.33%; overall average ASR 53.11% / RR 4.67%
RQ3 Benchmark 1 per-model average (ASR/RR): Claude 3.7 Sonnet 56.11%/8.89%; GPT-4o 40.00%/1.11%; DeepSeek-V3 0324 61.11%/10.00%; LLaMA3.1-70B 40.56%/3.34%; Gemini 2.5 Pro 67.78%/0.00%
RQ3 Benchmark 1: Exploitation via Malicious External Resources ASR exceeds Tool Poisoning Attack and Puppet Attack by 57.31% and 1,299.25% respectively (paper's own relative-increase figures)

RQ3 Benchmark 2 (Table 4, per authors' text): Cherry Studio average RR 33.89% across all attack vectors (51.67% specifically for Malicious External Resources); Copilot-MCP average RR 0.56% (0.00% for Puppet Attack and Malicious External Resources, 1.67% for Tool Poisoning); Cline vs Claude Desktop under Puppet Attack: both ~6.67% RR but ASR 5.00% vs 31.67% respectively; under Malicious External Resources, Cline ASR 93.33% vs Copilot-MCP ASR 60.00% despite both barely detecting the attack

45.0% of user-study participants willing to use MCP-enabled AI agents with sensitive data (personal notes, code); 45.0% (9/20 with hands-on MCP experience) admit neglecting warning dialogs after initial use; 80% believe platform providers should be security gatekeepers; at least 70.0% (14/20) say professional-looking platform trust signals reduce their vigilance
baselines_compared: Five LLM backbones compared against each other under identical attack conditions (fixed Cline client): Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3 0324, LLaMA3.1-70B, Gemini 2.5 Pro
Five MCP clients compared against each other under identical attack conditions (fixed Claude 3.7 Sonnet backbone): Cherry Studio, Claude Desktop, Cline, Copilot-MCP, Cursor
Three MCP aggregator platforms compared for audit robustness: Smithery.ai, MCP.so, Glama
